questions.prompt: How often does a @BeforeMethod hook run in a class with three @Test methods?
options: Once, before the first test only
Three times — once before each @Test method
Once, after the whole class finishes
Never, unless you call it from the test
questions.explanation: @BeforeMethod runs before every @Test method, so three tests means it runs three times. That is why it is the right place to open a fresh browser per test.

questions.prompt: What is the correct nesting order, outermost to innermost?
options: They all run at the same time in random order
questions.explanation: @BeforeSuite runs once for the whole run, @BeforeClass once per class, and @BeforeMethod before each test. Outer setup wraps inner setup; the after-hooks mirror this in reverse.

questions.prompt: Why open the driver in @BeforeMethod rather than inside each @Test?
options: It makes the browser open faster
It keeps setup out of the test body and guarantees a fresh browser for every test automatically
It is the only place TestNG allows a WebDriver
It prevents you from ever needing assertions
questions.explanation: Putting driver setup in @BeforeMethod removes copy-pasted boilerplate from each test and ensures every test — including ones you add later — starts with a clean browser.

questions.prompt: What happens if you never call driver.quit() in @AfterMethod?
options: Each test leaks its browser process, piling up zombie windows that eat memory
TestNG quits the browser for you automatically
The next test reuses the same browser with no downside
questions.explanation: Without teardown, every test leaves its browser running. Over a long suite these orphaned processes accumulate and exhaust the machine's memory.
